coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on quantum dots and boronic acid-functionalized MOF for detection of transferrin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Minghao
Pei, Fubin
Hu, Wei
Lei, Wu
Wang, Jiang
Tong, Zhaoyang
Liu, Bing
Hao, Qingli
Mu, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and rapid detection of transferrin (TrF) is useful in maintenance of human health. A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, red cadmium telluride quantum dots, and boronic acid-functionalized MOF (N-CDs/r-CdTe@MOF-B(OH)2@MIP, or NTZ@MIP) for the detection of TrF was constructed via the sol–gel process. Boric acid groups in the MOF-B(OH)2 carrier improved covalent binding to TrF through affinity interactions. MOF-B(OH)2 also provided a large specific surface area and porosity that increased the loading of quantum dots (QDs) and improved mass transfer efficiency and analytical sensitivity. N-CDs and r-CdTe acted as fluorophores that emitted at 448 nm and 642 nm, respectively, in the presence of TrF, which facilitated ratiometric fluorescence detection. Under optimized conditions, TrF could be detected in the range 0.05–15 µM, with a lower limit of detection of 0.025 µM. The NTZ@MIP sensor produced satisfactory results in the analysis of TrF-fortified samples of human urine and serum, with recoveries of 93.2 % and 99.6 %, respectively. A smartphone-based TrF sensing platform using NTZ@MIP was also designed and achieved satisfactory results, suggesting that this ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor has promising applications in on-site detection of TrF.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Boronic-acid functionalized MOF
Ratiometric fluorescence
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Intelligent sensor
Transferrin

Analysis of TrF-fortified urine and serum
TrF detection via smartphone sensing platform
Stability, repeatability, and reusability of the NTZ@MIP
Application of NTZ@MIP to urine and serum
TrF detection via smartphone sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of MOF-B(OH)2 (A), NTZ@MIP (B), NTZ@NIP (C). The corresponding elemental mappings of NTZ@MIP (D). (E) FTIR spectra of MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP, NTZ@NIP. (F) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP. (G) XRD patterns of MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP, NTZ@NIP.
XPS survey spectra of N-CDs (A). High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s for N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of MOF-B(OH)2, N-CDs and r-CdTe. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NTZ@MIP, NTZ@NIP, NTZ@MIP + TrF and NTZ@NIP + TrF. (C) The fluorescence excitation spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of the NTZ@MIP, UV–vis absorption spectrum of TrF. (D) and (E) Fluorescence emission decay curves of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@MIP + TrF (N-CDs,448 nm). (F) and (G) Fluorescence emission decay curves of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@MIP + TrF (r-CdTe,642 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of (A) NTZ@MIP and (B) NZT@NIP under different concentrations of TrF (excited at 350 nm, TrF concentrations: 0.05, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15 µM). The Stern-Volmer plots of (C) NTZ@MIP and (D) NZT@NIP. (Error bar = SD, n = 3).
Schematic illustration of synthetic process of NTZ@MIP.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on quantum dots and boronic acid-functionalized MOF for detection of transferrin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094 Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and rapid detection of transferrin (TrF) is useful in maintenance of human health. A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on nitrogen co-doped carbon dots, red cadmium telluride quantum dots, and boronic acid-functionalized MOF (N-CDs/r-CdTe@MOF-B(OH)2@MIP, or NTZ@MIP) for the detection of TrF was constructed via the sol–gel process. Boric acid groups in the MOF-B(OH)2 carrier improved covalent binding to TrF through affinity interactions. MOF-B(OH)2 also provided a large specific surface area and porosity that increased the loading of quantum dots (QDs) and improved mass transfer efficiency and analytical sensitivity. N-CDs and r-CdTe acted as fluorophores that emitted at 448 nm and 642 nm, respectively, in the presence of TrF, which facilitated ratiometric fluorescence detection. Under optimized conditions, TrF could be detected in the range 0.05–15 µM, with a lower limit of detection of 0.025 µM. The NTZ@MIP sensor produced satisfactory results in the analysis of TrF-fortified samples of human urine and serum, with recoveries of 93.2 % and 99.6 %, respectively. A smartphone-based TrF sensing platform using NTZ@MIP was also designed and achieved satisfactory results, suggesting that this ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor has promising applications in on-site detection of TrF.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Transferrin (TrF) is a glycoprotein responsible for iron transport in the body and is a recognized concentration-dependent biomarker [1] for various diseases including thalassemia, bacterial infection, diabetes, and cancer [2]. The accurate and rapid determination of TrF is crucial for the prevention and early diagnosis of such diseases. Currently, immunosensors [3,4], high performance liquid chromatography [5,6], and capillary electrophoresis [7,8] are the main methods for detecting TrF, but these are costly and require highly trained technicians and specialized instrumentation.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are biomolecular three-dimensional recognition materials that are produced by the self-assembly of functional monomers and template molecules. Polymerization proceeds by the addition of crosslinkers and initiators. [9,10]. The template molecule is then removed by elution, leaving a MIP with a cavity binding site that exactly matches the size, shape, and structure of the analyte of interest [11]. MIPs combine high sensitivity and selectivity with simple preparation and are extensively used in the fields of food safety [12], analytical chemistry [13], biomedicine [14], environmental science [15], and biosensing [16].

Detection methods using single wavelength fluorescence analysis are susceptible to environmental and instrumental interferences that can be overcome by use of ratiometric fluorescence techniques. Ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensors have been widely used in recent years because of their inherent stability, sensitivity, self-calibration, and immunity to interference. In recent years, carbon QDs have been widely applied in various fields such as biosensing due to their excellent optical properties, good water solubility, low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Nitrogen doping can modify the electronic structure of carbon QDs, enhance their fluorescence performance and chemical stability, making them more suitable for the biosensing field. Meanwhile, CdTe QDs are favored in the biosensing field due to their good biocompatibility. Moreover, N-CDs have similar excitation wavelengths to CdTe, making them very suitable as ratio fluorescence dual signals. Du et al. [17] constructed a dual-template ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor for the highly sensitive and selective detection of methyl eugenol and aristolochic acid A, while Xu et al. [18] constructed a ratiometric fluorescence probe for aflatoxin B1 detection.

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of crystalline porous materials with periodic network structures formed by the self-assembled bridging of inorganic metal centers with organic ligands [19]. They are widely used in fluorescence imprinted sensors due to their high specific surface area, high porosity, abundant active centers, and tunable structures [20,21]. Boric acid groups have been reported to covalently combine with molecules containing cis-diols to form borate esters [22–24], so MIPs using boric acid groups as functional monomers have been favored for the selective recognition of TrF. Zhang et al. [25] developed a magnetic fluorescent MIP sensor modified with boric acid for the detection and separation of TrF. Boronic acid-functionalized MOFs have been used to separate glycoproteins [26], but they were not selective. Thus, a molecularly imprinted sensor combined with a boronic acid-functionalized MOF could make use of the high specific surface area and porosity of the MOF blot carrier for detecting glycoproteins with greatly improved selectivity. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing has also benefitted from the continued development of smartphones, with their increasing capacity for image acquisition and rapid analysis and processing. Their ease of use and portability promise a wide range of applications in this field.

This study describes a simple and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor (NTZ@MIP) constructed from a boronic acid-functionalized MOF (MOF-B(OH)2), N co-doped carbon dots (N-CDs), and red chromium telluride (r-CdTe) quantum dots (QDs). TrF detection sensitivity is greatly improved by the N-CDs and r-CdTe, which considerably reduce interferences with the response signal. The high specific surface area of MOF-B(OH)2 not only serves as the carrier imprint, but also facilitates increased loading of QDs, which enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of NTZ@MIP for TrF. NTZ@MIP is successfully used to detect TrF in fortified human urine and serum samples. Development of a portable sensing device for the detection of TrF is also described. This sensor platform has a range of potential applications in TrF detection, including on-site field applications.

Cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), 3-mercaptopropyl acid (MPA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), tellurium powder, citric acid, ethanediamine, myoglobin (Mb), ovalbumin (OVA), ethanol, 3-aminopropyrtriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS), terephthalic acid (BDC), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. 4-Carboxyphenylboronic acid (CPB), transferrin (TrF), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co. Ltd., and cytochrome c (Cyt c) from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd.

UV signal analyses used a spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra using a Nicolet iS50 (ThermoFisher, USA), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250Xi (ThermoFisher, USA), while a Tecnai G2 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, USA) was used to characterize the materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-CDs, CdTe QDs, and MOF-B(OH)2
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed protocols for the synthesis of N-CDs [27], CdTe QDs [28,29], and MOF-B(OH)2 [30] are provided in the Supplementary Material.

MOF-B(OH)2 (10 mg) was dispersed by ultrasonication in 2 mL of 10 mM PBS (pH 8.5), 5 mg TrF was added and the mixture was stirred at 400 r/min for 3 h at 25 °C to encourage binding. The TrF-bound MOF-B(OH)2 was then mixed with 8 mL anhydrous ethanol to which N-CDs (100 µL) and r-CdTe (90 µL) were added. Following addition of 150 µL TEOS and 100 µL NH3·H2O, the mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 12 h, at which point the NTZ@MIP was centrifuged and washed three times with deionized water to remove unreacted compounds. The template was then eluted from the NTZ@MIP using 10 % SDS until template could not be detected at 280 nm in the supernatant, then the NTZ@MIP was freeze-dried. Preparation of non-imprinted polymer (NTZ@NIP) was the same as for NTZ@MIP, except that the template molecule was omitted.

TrF was determined as follows: various concentrations of TrF were added to a 1.0 mg·mL−1 solution of NTZ@MIP for 1 min (v/v 1:1) in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.5), then fluorescence spectra were acquired across the range 410–750 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Data were obtained from three replicate experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of TrF-fortified urine and serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human urine and serum purchased from Solarbio Life Sciences were fortified with TrF to simulate incurred samples and to evaluate the performance of NTZ@MIP. Urine samples were fortified with TrF (1, 5, and 10 µM) and analyzed without further treatment. Serum was diluted 50-fold with PBS before fortification with 1, 5, and 10 µM of TrF. Samples were assayed for TrF using fluorescence detection as described in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TrF detection via smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: A detailed description of the smartphone-based sensing platform can be found in the Supplementary Material.

As shown in Scheme 1 , a ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor NTZ@MIP was prepared via the sol–gel method. N-CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method and r-CdTe was prepared via the one-pot condensation–reflux method. MOF-B(OH)2 was prepared from BDC and CPB. TrF was combined with MOF-B(OH)2 via boronation affinity, then the MIP was formed under the action of TEOS and NH3·H2O. Elution of the template with SDS created recognition cavities in the NTZ@MIP that matched the shape and size of TrF. Addition of TrF to NTZ@MIP quenched fluorescence at 448 and 642 nm, showing that the MIP can be used as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detection of TrF.

The morphologies of N-CDs, r-CdTe, MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP, and NTZ@NIP were investigated using TEM. The diameters of N-CDs and r-CdTe were approximately 5 and 4 nm, respectively (Fig. S1A and S1B). The morphology of MOF-B(OH)2 is shown in Fig. 1 A, exhibiting a mean size of 100 nm. The mean size of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP (180 nm) was larger than MOF-B(OH)2, implying that imprinted polymers had been successfully prepared (Fig. 1B and Fig. 1C). Elemental image mapping of NTZ@MIP was performed. This revealed the characteristic elements B, N, Si, Cd, Te, and Zr, again indicating the successful preparation of NTZ@MIP (Fig. 1D).

The functional groups on the surfaces of MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP, and NTZ@NIP were analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy to verify the creation of imprinted polymers. In Fig. 1E, the 1585.1 cm−1 and 1398.4 cm−1 peaks were attributed to the stretching vibration of O=C–O, while 1154.4 cm−1 and 1017.2 cm−1 were attributed to the vibratory peaks of B–O–H and B–O–O–H, indicating that the boronic acid groups had been successfully introduced. Peaks at 746.1 cm−1 and 657.4 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of Zr–O, indicating the successful preparation of MOF-B(OH)2. FT-IR spectra of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP were almost the same, with 1087.4 cm−1 attributable to the asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si, and 800.3 cm−1 and 466.9 cm−1 to the stretching vibration of Si-O and Si-O-Si, respectively. This verified the successful construction of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP [31].

As shown in Fig. 1F, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP were 16.58 and 4.07 m2/g, respectively. Compared with NTZ@NIP, NTZ@MIP has a larger specific surface area, which is mainly attributed to the abundant imprinted cavities on the surface of NTZ@MIP. The crystal structures of MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP, and NTZ@NIP were investigated using XRD (Fig. 1G). MOF-B(OH)2 XRD peaks were consistent with previous reports [32]. NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP exhibited similar peaks that were much weaker than MOF-B(OH)2. Only one characteristic diffraction peak was observed (at 2θ = 23°). This belonged to the amorphous SiO2 imprinted layer and covered the MOF-B(OH)2 peak, which is consistent with previous reports [33]. This analysis confirmed the creation of imprinted layers.

Fig. 2 A shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of the N-CDs. Fig. 2B shows binding energy peaks at 284.8, 286, and 288.2 eV in C1s, which are attributed to C–C, C–O, and C=O respectively. In N1s, the bonds N–O, N–H, and N-C produced binding energy peaks at 401.5, 400.5, and 399.5 eV, respectively (Fig. 2C). The peaks in O1s at 531 and 533 eV are attributed to C=O and C–O (Fig. 2D). This indicates the successful preparation of N-CDs.

Optical property analysis showed that N-CDs and r-CdTe exhibited fluorescence peaks at 448 and 630 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 A; excitation wavelength 350 nm). Notably, the fluorescence spectrum of MOF-B(OH)2 was a straight line under these conditions, indicating that it had little effect on this analysis. Interestingly, NTZ@MIP exhibited a redshift at 642 nm, which was attributed to r-CdTe being prone to aggregation in the imprinting layer, resulting in changes in the microenvironment. To verify the feasibility of NTZ@MIP for detecting TrF, changes in the fluorescence intensity of the imprinted polymers NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP upon binding to TrF were investigated. Fluorescence of both polymers was quenched at 448 and 642 nm after addition of the same concentration of TrF (Fig. 3B). Quenching of NTZ@MIP was greater than NTZ@NIP, so the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by TrF was investigated. Proposed fluorescence quenching mechanisms have included fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), internal filtering effect (IFE), static quenching, and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [34]. UV absorption by TrF occurred at 280 nm, while the fluorescence emission spectrum of NTZ@MIP was between 410 and 750 nm (Fig. 3C). Since there is no overlap between these wavelengths, quenching cannot be caused by FRET. TrF UV absorption at 280 nm also does not overlap with fluorescence excitation at 350 nm, so fluorescence quenching was not due to IFE. Static quenching occurs when the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore remains unchanged [35]. When TrF was added, the fluorescence lifetime of NTZ@MIP at 448 nm decreased from 9.84 to 8.67 ns (Fig. 3D and 3E), and at 642 nm it decreased from 30.04 to 23.49 ns (Fig. 3F and 3G). This excludes the possibility of static quenching. Previous reports [36] show that when the cis-diol in TrF reacts with boronic acid groups in NTZ@MIP via an affinity reaction, the distance between the two is shortened, leading to direct transfer of excited electrons in N-CDs and r-CdTe to the lowest energy level of unoccupied orbitals in TrF. Thus, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of TrF can be attributed to PET.

Maximizing the efficiency of MIP imprinting is crucial to the performance of the sensor. In particular, the quantity of TEOS added is important: Too much can create an imprinted layer that is too thick and difficult to elute, while too little TEOS can produce a thin layer that is easily broken. The results of TEOS optimization are shown in Fig. S2A. Optimum imprinting was achieved with 150 µL TEOS and 12 h reaction time, which also affected the thickness of the imprinted layer (Fig. S2B). The proportions of N-CDs and r-CdTe were also optimized to produce an appropriate fluorescence peak height ratio. During synthesis of NTZ@MIP, N-CDs was fixed at 100 µL while the quantity of r-CdTe was varied (Fig. S2C). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 reached its maximum (indicating optimum blotting efficiency) when the N-CDs:r-CdTe volume ratio was 100:85. To improve the sensitivity of the NTZ@MIP response, the pH of the detection environment and the concentration of NTZ@MIP were optimized and the response time of NTZ@MIP to TrF was measured. The ability of NTZ@MIP to bind specifically to TrF is affected by the pH of the environment. The pH was evaluated in the range 7.0–8.5 and the ratio (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 was highest at pH 7.5 (Fig. S2D). This is because under this pH condition, the activity of TrF is the highest, and can better bind to NTZ@MIP. Similarly, (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 reached its maximum when the concentration of NTZ@MIP was 1.0 mg·mL−1 (Fig. S2E). Under these conditions, the response time of NTZ@MIP to TrF was 1 min (Fig. S2F).

Changes in fluorescence intensity of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP under varying TrF concentrations were studied to verify the response of the sensor. Increasing TrF concentration from 0.05 to 15 µM produced a downward trend in fluorescence emission at 448 nm that was more pronounced with NTZ@MIP. Crucially, across this concentration range, NTZ@MIP fluorescence at 642 nm dropped considerably, while it remained largely unchanged for NTZ@NIP (Fig. 4 A and 4B). This was mainly attributed to the fact that the NTZ@MIP surface has imprinting sites that match the size, shape and structure of TrF, while the NTZ@NIP surface does not have any imprinting sites. Therefore, the fluorescence decay rate of NTZ@MIP at 448 nm was faster than that of NTZ@NIP. At lower concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of NTZ@NIP at 642 nm remains basically unchanged. This is mainly because compared with r-CdTe, N-CDs have a higher response sensitivity, and thus can achieve fluorescence response to TrF at a lower concentration. There was a strong linear relationship between (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 of NTZ@MIP and TrF concentration in the range 0.05–15 µM (Fig. 4C and 4D), defined by y = 0.0371x + 0.9912 and an R2 value of 0.997. This correlation for NTZ@NIP was y = 0.0195x + 1.0252, with an R2 of 0.992. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.025 µM TrF based on LOD = 3σ/k [37]. The imprinting factor (IF) was calculated as 1.9, indicating that NTZ@MIP has a good recognition ability for TrF. Thus, NTZ@MIP was shown to be capable of recognizing TrF under 365 nm UV irradiation (28 W), exhibiting greater changes in color than NTZ@NIP as TrF concentration increased (Fig. 4A and 4B).

As a comparison, a single-signal fluorescent probe for detecting TrF was prepared (N-CDs@MOF-B(OH)2@MIP, namely NZ@MIP). Its fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as TrF concentration increased (Fig. S3A and S3B). The NZ@MIP (F448)/(F448)0 ratio was linearly dependent on TrF concentration in the range 0.1–15 µM, defined by y = 0.0672x + 1.0052 and an R2 value of 0.995. LOD is 0.03 μM. Compared with NZ@MIP, NTZ@MIP exhibits higher sensitivity, which might be related to the self-calibration feature of ratiometric fluorescence. Furthermore, the NTZ@MIP ratiometric fluorescence assay produces larger changes in color than NZ@MIP. This result indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (NTZ@MIP) has more advantages than the single-signal fluorescence sensor (NZ@MIP) in detecting TrF.

The selectivity of NTZ@MIP recognition was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratios (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 of NTZ@MIP and NTZ@NIP in the presence of the TrF-analogous proteins OVA, BHb, Mb, Cyt c, and HPR (Fig. S4). These proteins produced weak fluorescence responses in NTZ@MIP compared to TrF. This selectivity for TrF can be attributed to the dedicated recognition site in NTZ@MIP that complements the size and shape of the TrF target.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability, and reusability of the NTZ@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Stability, repeatability, and reusability are crucial for a sensor to find widespread applications. The stability of NTZ@MIP was investigated by measuring its ratiometric response over time. (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 did not change significantly after storage at 4 °C for 20 days, indicating satisfactory stability (Fig. S5A). Repeatability was investigated by measuring the fluorescence ratio across eight samples. This did not change, demonstrating the satisfactory repeatability of TrF analysis using NTZ@MIP (Fig. S5B). The reusability of NTZ@MIP was evaluated by removing the bound TrF and recombining it with the MIP five times. As shown in Fig. S5C, after five recombination experiments, (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 decreased only slightly, after five removal experiments, (F642/F448)/(F642/F448)0 showed a slight increase. This might be attributed to the fact that TrF in the sensor could not be completely removed. The above results indicated that that NTZ@MIP has satisfactory reusability. Thus, initial analyses suggest that NTZ@MIP has excellent potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NTZ@MIP to urine and serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the performance of NTZ@MIP in analyzing biological samples, commercially available human urine and serum were fortified with 1, 5, and 10 µM of TrF. The recoveries of the imprinted sensor based on NTZ@MIP ranged from 93.2 % to 99.6 %, with RSD < 3.2 % (Table S1). The obtained results were compared with those derived from the HPLC-UV method, revealing a high degree of consistency between the two sets of data. The above results indicated that the molecularly imprinted sensor based on NTZ@MIP was capable of fulfilling the analytical requirements for TrF. Moreover, TrF recoveries using the smartphone sensing platform ranged from 94 % to 103.2 %, with RSD < 3.3 % (Table S2). These data show that the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor NTZ@MIP meets the requirements of detection of TrF in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TrF detection via smartphone sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The smartphone-based sensing system consisted of a 365 nm UV lamp, cuvette, filter, smartphone, camera obscura, and portable power source (Fig. S6). The smartphone image capture function converts the fluorescent colors to corresponding RGB values. The R/B ratio exhibited a linear relationship with TrF concentration across the range 0.2–15 µM (y = 0.0372x + 0.7276; R2 = 0.988; LOD = 0.14 µM). This demonstrates that detection of TrF using a smartphone-based sensing system is feasible for on-site applications.

As shown in Table S3, the NTZ@MIP sensor prepared was compared with the detection method of TrF currently developed. The NEB@MIP sensor exhibited high sensitivity (with a detection limit of 25 nM) within the range of 0.05–15 µM. Compared with other methods, this method takes advantage of the large surface area and high porosity of MOF, enabling it to load more N-CDs and r-CdTe on its surface, thereby achieving dual-fluorescence signal detection, effectively reducing background interference and improving detection sensitivity. Moreover, the use of a detection platform based on smartphones makes it possible for on-site rapid detection of TrF, meeting the practical needs of portability and real-time analysis.

A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor (NTZ@MIP) capable of rapidly and selectively detecting TrF was prepared. Optimization of the proportions of N-CDs and r-CdTe enhanced the fluorescence color changes produced by NTZ@MIP, allowing TrF to be detected by the naked eye. Combining the advantages of QDs and MOF-B(OH)2, NTZ@MIP binds to TrF within one minute and exhibits excellent linearity and sensitivity (0.025 μM TrF). The highly specific recognition sites in the imprinted layer enabled the application of NTZ@MIP to TrF detection in fortified urine and human serum, with satisfactory results. A smartphone-based TrF-sensing platform using NTZ@MIP achieved similar satisfactory results, opening possibilities for on-site applications for TrF detection.

Minghao Ma: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Fubin Pei: Validation. Wei Hu: Software, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wu Lei: Resources. Jiang Wang: Resources. Zhaoyang Tong: Resources. Bing Liu: Supervision, Resources. Qingli Hao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Xihui Mu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2022-06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116378.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
